preamble.summary: A royal psalm attributed to Solomon (or written for Solomon by David) that envisions the ideal king whose reign embodies perfect justice, compassion for the poor, universal dominion, and abundance that mirrors Eden. The psalm closes Book II of the Psalter with a doxology (verses 18-19) and a colophon noting the end of the prayers of David son of Jesse. It is the most expansive portrait of the messianic king in the Psalter.
preamble.remarkable: Psalm 72 describes a king whose justice produces cosmic fertility — when the king judges rightly, the mountains bear peace and the hills produce righteousness (verse 3). This is not metaphor for the ancient Israelite mind; it is theology. The king's justice and the land's fruitfulness are connected through the covenant. The psalm reaches its most astonishing scope in verses 8-11, where the king's dominion extends from sea to sea and from the River to the ends of the earth, and all kings bow before him. No historical Israelite king achieved this, which is precisely why the psalm became messianic — it describes what should be, and Israel kept praying it forward. The tender attention to the poor and crushed (verses 12-14) places compassion at the center of royal power: the king's greatness is measured not by conquest but by whom he defends.
preamble.friction: The superscription li-Shlomoh can mean 'of Solomon,' 'for Solomon,' or 'concerning Solomon.' If David composed it for his son, verse 20 ('The prayers of David son of Jesse are ended') makes sense as a colophon. If Solomon wrote it about himself, the colophon was added by a later editor collecting Book II. The universal scope (verses 8-11) far exceeds Solomon's actual kingdom, leading many scholars to read this as idealized royal theology rather than historical description. In Jewish tradition, the psalm is messianic; in Christian tradition, it is applied to Christ.
preamble.connections: The language of verse 8 ('from sea to sea, from the River to the ends of the earth') reappears in Zechariah 9:10 in an explicitly messianic context. The concern for the poor echoes the covenant code (Exodus 22:21-27) and anticipates the prophetic critique of kings who failed this mandate (Jeremiah 22:13-17). The psalm's placement at the end of Book II creates a structural parallel with the doxologies that close Books I (Psalm 41:14), III (Psalm 89:53), IV (Psalm 106:48), and V (Psalm 150). The 'gold of Sheba' (verse 15) connects to the Solomon narrative in 1 Kings 10.

verses.text_hebrew: לִשְׁלֹמֹ֡ה אֱ‍ֽלֹהִ֗ים מִשְׁפָּטֶ֥יךָ לְמֶֽלֶךְ־תֵּ֑ן וְצִדְקָתְךָ֥ לְבֶן־מֶֽלֶךְ׃
verses.text_kjv: Give the king thy judgments, O God, and thy righteousness unto the king's son.
verses.rendering:
Of Solomon.

O God, give Your judgments to the king,
and Your righteousness to the king's son.
verses.expanded_rendering: The psalm opens as a prayer — not a statement of fact but a petition. The request is not for wisdom or military victory but for mishpatekha ('Your judgments') and tsidqatekha ('Your righteousness'). The king needs God's own capacity for justice transferred to him. The phrase ben melekh ('king's son') parallels melekh ('king') — this could refer to the crown prince or to the same king described from two angles: the reigning monarch who is also the heir of a dynasty.
translator_notes: The superscription li-Shlomoh is ambiguous: the preposition le- can mean 'of,' 'for,' 'to,' or 'concerning.' If this is a prayer by David for Solomon (as the colophon in verse 20 suggests), it is a father's intercession for his son's reign.

key_terms.semantic_range: judgment, justice, legal decision, ordinance, the act of ruling rightly
key_terms.note: Mishpat is the practical execution of justice — the decisions a ruler makes, the verdicts rendered at the gate. It is not abstract but concrete: who wins the case, who gets the land, who is protected. The prayer asks that the king's rulings mirror God's own.

verses.text_hebrew: יָדִ֣ין עַמְּךָ֣ בְצֶ֑דֶק וַעֲנִיֶּ֥יךָ בְמִשְׁפָּֽט׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall judge thy people with righteousness, and thy poor with judgment.
verses.rendering:
May he judge Your people with righteousness
and Your afflicted ones with justice.
verses.expanded_rendering: The parallelism is deliberate: 'Your people' is paired with 'Your afflicted ones' (aniyyekha). The poor are not a marginal concern — they are God's people, singled out for special attention. The king's primary responsibility is not foreign policy or military strategy but ensuring that the most vulnerable members of society receive fair treatment.
translator_notes: The word ani ('afflicted, poor, humble') describes those who are economically and socially powerless. In the Psalms, the aniyyim are consistently portrayed as God's particular concern — the ones He hears, defends, and vindicates.

verses.text_hebrew: יִשְׂא֤וּ הָרִ֓ים שָׁ֘ל֥וֹם לָעָ֑ם וּ֝גְבָע֗וֹת בִּצְדָקָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: The mountains shall bring peace to the people, and the little hills, by righteousness.
verses.rendering:
May the mountains bear peace for the people,
and the hills, through righteousness.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse makes a claim that modern readers may find strange but that is central to biblical theology: just rule produces natural abundance. When the king governs with righteousness, the mountains themselves bear shalom ('peace, well-being, wholeness') for the people. The land responds to the moral quality of its ruler. This is not superstition but covenant theology — the Deuteronomic tradition (Deuteronomy 28) explicitly links obedience to agricultural fertility and disobedience to drought and famine.
translator_notes: The verb yis'u ('may they bear, carry, lift up') is the same verb used for carrying a burden or bearing fruit. The mountains are portrayed as productive — their yield is peace. This imagery will reappear in Isaiah 55:12 where the mountains 'break forth into singing.'

verses.text_hebrew: יִשְׁפֹּ֤ט ׀ עֲ‍ֽנִיֵּי־עָ֗ם י֭וֹשִׁיעַ לִבְנֵ֣י אֶבְי֑וֹן וִֽידַכֵּ֣א עוֹשֵֽׁק׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall judge the poor of the people, he shall save the children of the needy, and shall break in pieces the oppressor.
verses.rendering:
May he vindicate the afflicted of the people,
save the children of the needy,
and crush the oppressor.
translator_notes: The verb yishpot here means not merely 'judge' but 'vindicate' — to render a verdict in favor of someone who has been wronged. The ideal king does not merely hear cases; he actively rescues (yoshia) the vulnerable and actively destroys (vidakke) those who exploit them. Justice is not passive.

verses.text_hebrew: יִ֭ירָא֣וּךָ עִם־שָׁ֑מֶשׁ וְלִפְנֵ֥י יָ֝רֵ֗חַ דּ֣וֹר דּוֹרִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: They shall fear thee as long as the sun and moon endure, throughout all generations.
verses.rendering:
May they fear You as long as the sun endures,
and before the moon, through all generations.
translator_notes: The subject shifts from the king to the people: 'may they fear You' — the king's just rule produces reverence for God. The temporal markers — 'as long as the sun,' 'before the moon,' 'through all generations' (dor dorim) — express permanence. The prayer is for an everlasting dynasty, a hope that no historical dynasty fulfilled.

verses.text_hebrew: יֵ֭רֵד כְּמָטָ֣ר עַל־גֵּ֑ז כִּ֝רְבִיבִ֗ים זַרְזִ֥יף אָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall come down like rain upon the mown grass: as showers that water the earth.
verses.rendering:
May he come down like rain on mown grass,
like showers that drench the earth.
verses.expanded_rendering: The king is compared to rain — the most precious resource in an arid land. The word gez ('mown grass, freshly cut meadow') pictures a field that has been harvested and lies exposed; rain on gez means renewal after cutting, life after apparent destruction. The word zarzif ('dripping, drenching') is rare and onomatopoetic — it sounds like water dripping. The king's reign is supposed to feel like relief, like water on parched ground.
translator_notes: This is one of the most beautiful images in the Psalter. The comparison of a just king to rain reverses the usual biblical metaphor of storm-God as warrior; here, God's representative is gentle, nourishing, and life-giving. The same image appears in 2 Samuel 23:4 in David's last words.

verses.text_hebrew: יִפְרַח־בְּיָמָ֥יו צַדִּ֑יק וְרֹ֥ב שָׁ֝ל֗וֹם עַד־בְּלִ֥י יָרֵֽחַ׃
verses.text_kjv: In his days shall the righteous flourish; and abundance of peace so long as the moon endureth.
verses.rendering:
In his days may the righteous flourish
and peace abound until the moon is no more.
translator_notes: The verb yifrach ('may he flourish, sprout, blossom') continues the agricultural imagery — the righteous bloom like plants under the king's just rule. The phrase ad beli yare'ach ('until there is no moon') means 'forever' — as long as the created order endures.

verses.text_hebrew: וְ֭יֵרְדְּ מִיָּ֣ם עַד־יָ֑ם וּ֝מִנָּהָ֗ר עַד־אַפְסֵי־אָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall have dominion also from sea to sea, and from the river unto the ends of the earth.
verses.rendering:
May he rule from sea to sea,
and from the River to the ends of the earth.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse describes universal dominion. 'From sea to sea' (mi-yam ad yam) stretches from the Mediterranean to the eastern sea (possibly the Persian Gulf or the cosmic ocean). 'From the River' (min-nahar) refers to the Euphrates, the traditional northern boundary of the promised land (Genesis 15:18). 'To the ends of the earth' (ad afsei arets) removes all limits. No Israelite king, including Solomon at his greatest extent, ruled this territory. The psalm is praying for something that does not yet exist — and Israel will keep praying it until it does.
translator_notes: Zechariah 9:10 quotes this verse nearly verbatim in an explicitly messianic prophecy about a coming king who rides on a donkey. The psalm's language became the vocabulary of messianic hope in later Jewish and Christian tradition.

key_terms.semantic_range: ends, extremities, limits, the farthest reaches of the inhabited world
key_terms.note: Efes means 'end, nothing, cessation' — the place where the earth runs out. The phrase describes the absolute boundary of the created world. The king's dominion is coextensive with creation itself.

verses.text_hebrew: לְ֭פָנָיו יִכְרְע֣וּ צִיִּ֑ים וְ֝אֹיְבָ֗יו עָפָ֥ר יְלַחֵֽכוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: They that dwell in the wilderness shall bow before him; and his enemies shall lick the dust.
verses.rendering:
Before him the desert dwellers will kneel,
and his enemies will lick the dust.
translator_notes: The tsiyyim ('desert dwellers, inhabitants of arid places') represent the most remote and untamed peoples — even they submit. The phrase afar yelachekhu ('they will lick the dust') is an ancient Near Eastern image of complete submission: prostrating so low that one's face touches the ground. It appears in Micah 7:17 and Isaiah 49:23.

verses.text_hebrew: מַלְכֵ֤י תַרְשִׁ֣ישׁ וְ֭אִיִּים מִנְחָ֣ה יָשִׁ֑יבוּ מַלְכֵ֥י שְׁבָ֥א וּ֝סְבָ֗א אֶשְׁכָּ֥ר יַקְרִֽיבוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The kings of Tarshish and of the isles shall bring presents; the kings of Sheba and Seba shall offer gifts.
verses.rendering:
The kings of Tarshish and the coastlands will bring tribute;
the kings of Sheba and Seba will present gifts.
verses.expanded_rendering: The four geographical references span the known world. Tarshish is in the far west (likely southern Spain or Sardinia), the iyyim ('coastlands, islands') represent the Mediterranean maritime world, Sheba is in the south (modern Yemen), and Seba is in Africa (possibly Ethiopia or Sudan). From every direction, kings come bearing minchah ('tribute, offering') and eshkar ('gift, tribute payment'). The distinction between the two terms is significant: minchah is the standard word for tribute or grain offering, while eshkar is a rarer word suggesting a gift brought to curry favor with a superior.
translator_notes: The arrival of the queen of Sheba with gifts in 1 Kings 10 is the historical echo of this poetic vision. The psalm imagines what happened once under Solomon happening perpetually under the ideal king.

verses.text_hebrew: וְיִשְׁתַּחֲווּ־ל֥וֹ כׇל־מְלָכִ֑ים כׇּל־גּ֝וֹיִ֗ם יַעַבְדֽוּהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Yea, all kings shall fall down before him: all nations shall serve him.
verses.rendering:
Let all kings bow down before him;
let all nations serve him.
translator_notes: The universality is absolute: kol melakhim ('all kings') and kol goyim ('all nations'). The verb yishtachavu ('they will bow down, prostrate themselves') is the standard word for worship in the Hebrew Bible — the line between political submission and religious worship is intentionally blurred. In bowing to this king, the nations are acknowledging the God who appointed him.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּֽי־יַ֭צִּיל אֶבְי֣וֹן מְשַׁוֵּ֑עַ וְ֝עָנִ֗י וְֽאֵין־עֹזֵ֥ר לֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: For he shall deliver the needy when he crieth; the poor also, and him that hath no helper.
verses.rendering:
For he will deliver the needy who cries out,
the afflicted who has no helper.
verses.expanded_rendering: The word ki ('for, because') introduces the reason all nations serve this king: not his military power or wealth, but his compassion. The evyon meshavvea ('the needy one who cries out') and the ani ve-ein ozer lo ('the afflicted one who has no helper') are the most vulnerable people in any society — those with no advocate, no patron, no recourse. The ideal king's legitimacy rests on his treatment of precisely these people. This is revolutionary political theology: power exists to serve the powerless.
translator_notes: This verse provides the theological justification for the universal dominion described in verses 8-11. The king deserves to rule the world because he rescues the helpless. In prophetic literature, Israel's actual kings are condemned precisely for failing this standard (see Ezekiel 34, Jeremiah 22).

verses.text_hebrew: יָ֭חֹס עַל־דַּ֣ל וְאֶבְי֑וֹן וְנַפְשׁ֖וֹת אֶבְיוֹנִ֣ים יוֹשִֽׁיעַ׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall spare the poor and needy, and shall save the souls of the needy.
verses.rendering:
He will have pity on the poor and needy,
and the lives of the needy he will save.
translator_notes: The verb yachos ('he will have pity, spare, show compassion') describes an emotional response — the king does not merely adjudicate cases involving the poor; he feels for them. The repeated emphasis on evyonim ('needy ones') in this verse drives the point home through repetition.

verses.text_hebrew: מִתּ֣וֹךְ וּ֭מֵחָמָס יִגְאַ֣ל נַפְשָׁ֑ם וְיֵיקַ֖ר דָּמָ֣ם בְּעֵינָֽיו׃
verses.text_kjv: He shall redeem their soul from deceit and violence: and precious shall their blood be in his sight.
verses.rendering:
From oppression and violence he will redeem their lives,
and their blood will be precious in his eyes.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb yig'al ('he will redeem') is the language of the go'el — the kinsman-redeemer who buys back family members from slavery or avenges their blood. The king acts as go'el for the entire nation's poor. The final phrase — veyeqar damam be-einav ('their blood will be precious in his eyes') — is devastating in its simplicity: the king will regard the lives of the poor as valuable. In a world where the blood of the powerless was routinely spilled without consequence, this is the most radical claim the psalm makes.
translator_notes: The word tokh is often emended to 'oppression' or 'exploitation' (from a root meaning 'to oppress'), though some read it as 'deceit.' Paired with chamas ('violence'), it describes the two primary ways the powerful exploit the weak: through fraud and through force.

key_terms.semantic_range: to redeem, to act as kinsman-redeemer, to buy back, to reclaim, to avenge
key_terms.note: The go'el is the family member responsible for buying back relatives sold into slavery, reclaiming family land, and avenging murdered kin (Ruth 4, Leviticus 25). When the king 'redeems' the poor, he takes on this intimate family role for the entire nation.

verses.text_hebrew: וִיחִ֗י וְיִתֶּן־ל֗וֹ מִזְּהַ֥ב שְׁבָ֑א וְיִתְפַּלֵּ֥ל בַּ֝עֲד֗וֹ תָמִ֥יד כׇּל־הַיּ֥וֹם יְבָרֲכֶֽנְהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: And he shall live, and to him shall be given of the gold of Sheba: prayer also shall be made for him continually; and daily shall he be blessed.
translator_notes: The subject shifts rapidly: 'may he live' refers to the king, 'may gold be given to him' describes tribute, and 'may prayer be offered for him' shows the people praying for their king. The gold of Sheba connects to the Solomon narrative (1 Kings 10:2, 10). The phrase tamid kol hayyom ('continually, all day long') intensifies the blessing — the king is to be upheld by ceaseless intercession.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהִ֤י פִסַּת־בַּ֨ר ׀ בָּאָ֗רֶץ בְּרֹ֣אשׁ הָ֭רִים יִרְעַ֣שׁ כַּלְּבָנ֣וֹן פִּרְי֑וֹ וְיָצִ֥יצוּ מֵ֝עִ֗יר כְּעֵ֣שֶׂב הָאָֽרֶץ׃
verses.text_kjv: There shall be an handful of corn in the earth upon the top of the mountains; the fruit thereof shall shake like Lebanon: and they of the city shall flourish like grass of the earth.
verses.rendering:
May there be abundance of grain in the land,
on the tops of the mountains may it wave like Lebanon;
and may people blossom from the city
like grass of the earth.
verses.expanded_rendering: This verse describes supernatural fertility. Grain growing on mountaintops — normally unsuitable for agriculture — signals that the king's justice has healed the very landscape. The phrase yir'ash ka-Levanon piryo ('its fruit will rustle like Lebanon') compares grain fields to the great cedar forests of Lebanon — an image of overwhelming, almost excessive abundance. The final line — veyatsitsu me-ir ke-esev ha-arets ('they will blossom from the city like grass of the earth') — turns to population growth: people will proliferate like grass, unstoppable and abundant. The king's justice produces both agricultural and demographic blessing.
translator_notes: The word pissat is difficult. It may mean 'handful,' 'abundance,' or 'a cake/wafer' of grain. The context of overwhelming fertility supports 'abundance.' The image of grain on mountaintops is deliberately impossible under normal conditions — it signals the eschatological, Eden-like character of this king's reign.

verses.text_hebrew: יְהִ֤י שְׁמ֨וֹ ׀ לְֽעוֹלָ֗ם לִפְנֵי־שֶׁ֭מֶשׁ יִנּ֣וֹן שְׁמ֑וֹ וְיִתְבָּ֥רְכוּ ב֝֗וֹ כׇּל־גּוֹיִ֥ם יְאַשְּׁרֽוּהוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: His name shall endure for ever: his name shall be continued as long as the sun: and men shall be blessed in him: all nations shall call him blessed.
verses.expanded_rendering: The verb yinnon ('may it flourish, propagate, produce offspring') is related to nin ('offspring, posterity') — the king's name will not merely survive but will generate new life, new meaning, new blessing for future generations. The phrase veyitbarekhu vo kol goyim ('may all nations bless themselves by him') deliberately echoes the Abrahamic promise of Genesis 12:3 and 22:18. The king is the conduit through whom God's blessing reaches all peoples. This is the most explicitly messianic verse in the psalm — it connects the Davidic dynasty to the Abrahamic covenant and projects both into universal scope.
translator_notes: The LXX and Targum both read yinnon as a messianic title. The Talmud (Sanhedrin 98b) lists Yinnon as one of the names of the Messiah. The echo of the Abrahamic blessing ('all nations will be blessed in him') transforms the royal psalm into a covenant psalm — the king is the fulfillment of what God promised to Abraham.

key_terms.semantic_range: to propagate, to produce offspring, to continue, to flourish perpetually
key_terms.note: This rare verbal form became a messianic title in rabbinic tradition. The Talmud (Sanhedrin 98b) lists Yinnon among the possible names of the Messiah, based on this verse. The word carries the sense of perpetual, generative life — a name that keeps producing.

verses.text_hebrew: בָּר֤וּךְ ׀ יְהוָ֣ה אֱ֭לֹהִים אֱלֹהֵ֣י יִשְׂרָאֵ֑ל עֹשֵׂ֖ה נִפְלָא֣וֹת לְבַדּֽוֹ׃
verses.text_kjv: Blessed be the LORD God, the God of Israel, who only doeth wondrous things.
verses.rendering:
Blessed be the LORD God, the God of Israel,
who alone does wonders.
verses.expanded_rendering: The doxology begins. This is not part of the psalm proper but the liturgical conclusion to Book II of the Psalter. The phrase oseh nifl'aot levaddo ('who does wonders alone') affirms that everything described in the psalm — the ideal king, the cosmic justice, the universal peace — is ultimately God's work. No human king accomplishes these things by his own power. The 'wonders' (nifl'aot) are God's signature actions: the Exodus, the creation, the deliverances.
translator_notes: This doxology closes Book II (Psalms 42-72) just as Psalm 41:14 closes Book I. Each of the Psalter's five books ends with a doxology, and the final book (Book V) ends with five consecutive psalms of praise (146-150) as a grand doxology for the entire collection.

verses.text_hebrew: וּבָר֤וּךְ ׀ שֵׁ֥ם כְּבוֹד֗וֹ לְע֫וֹלָ֥ם וְיִמָּלֵ֣א כְ֭בוֹדוֹ אֶת־כׇּל־הָאָ֑רֶץ אָ֝מֵ֗ן ׀ וְאָמֵֽן׃
verses.text_kjv: And blessed be his glorious name for ever: and let the whole earth be filled with his glory; Amen, and Amen.
verses.expanded_rendering: The phrase shem kevodo ('the name of His glory') is not merely 'His glorious name' but 'the name that is His glory' — God's reputation, His manifest presence, His weight in the world. The prayer veyimmale khevodo et kol ha-arets ('may His glory fill all the earth') echoes the seraphim's cry in Isaiah 6:3 and the divine promise in Numbers 14:21. The double Amen seals the doxology and the entire second book of the Psalter.
translator_notes: The double Amen ('Amen and Amen') is the standard closing formula for the doxologies that end each book of the Psalter. Amen means 'truly, so be it, let it be established' — it is the congregation's affirmation of the prayer.

key_terms.semantic_range: glory, weight, honor, splendor, the manifest presence of God
key_terms.note: Kavod derives from a root meaning 'to be heavy, weighty.' God's glory is His weight — His substantive, undeniable presence in the world. The prayer that God's glory fill the whole earth envisions the entire creation becoming a temple, saturated with divine presence.

verses.text_hebrew: כָּלּ֥וּ תְפִלּ֑וֹת דָּ֝וִ֗ד בֶּן־יִשָֽׁי׃
verses.text_kjv: The prayers of David the son of Jesse are ended.
verses.rendering: Here conclude the prayers of David, Jesse's son.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is a colophon — an editorial note marking the end of a collection, not part of the psalm itself. It signals that the compiler of Book II understood the preceding psalms as the prayers of David. The fact that Davidic psalms appear after this point (in Books III-V) indicates that the Psalter was compiled in stages: Book II was closed with this note, and later editors added further Davidic material in subsequent collections.
translator_notes: The word kallu ('are finished, are ended, are completed') from kalah has a sense of finality and completion. This colophon is one of the clearest editorial fingerprints in the Psalter, revealing the hand of the collectors who shaped the book into its current five-part structure.
